cue: How do you say you're just eyeballing it?

es.literal: by the eye of a good cooper
es.meaning: By eyeball estimate; roughly, without measuring.
es.example: —¿Cuánta sal le pongo? —A ojo de buen cubero.
es.exampleTranslation: “How much salt do I add?” “Just eyeball it.”
es.regionNote: Submitted from lived Mexican usage — strongly current in MX, especially in cooking. The cubero was the barrel-maker whose trained eye gauged capacity. Bare 'a ojo' also works everywhere.

it.example: A occhio e croce ci vorranno due ore.
it.exampleTranslation: At a rough guess it'll take two hours.
it.regionNote: Kitchen split PARTLY RESOLVED: Treccani glosses the locuzione with estimating verbs — stimare, giudicare, valutare — and De Mauro's example is guessing someone's age, both supporting Claude's claim that 'a occhio e croce' leans toward estimates of quantity, time and value. Whether plain 'a occhio' is the natural kitchen form (metti il sale a occhio) is still unattested here — Italian reviewer to settle.

bridge.note: Both languages measure by eye, and both own the bare form — a ojo, a occhio — but their elaborations part company beautifully: Spanish summons a professional, the cooper whose trained eye sized barrels without a gauge, while Italian adds a cross from the loom. Weavers who lost their warp threads had to pick them back up by eye, held crossed against the weft — no measuring, hence the phrase, which traces to Latin ad oculum. A craftsman versus a craft, guarding the same guess.

interference.es_to_it: Calquing the cooper — 'all'occhio del buon bottaio' — is nonsense in Italian. In the kitchen say a occhio; for estimates, a occhio e croce.
interference.it_to_es: 'A ojo y cruz' is not Spanish. Bare 'a ojo' carries it everywhere; a ojo de buen cubero adds the flavor.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-16 from Mexican usage (cooking context). IT pairing chosen by Claude. 2026-07-18 CORRECTIONS: (1) etymology — Claude's 'Milanese silk weavers' contained invented detail; the loom/warp-thread origin and Latin ad oculum are corroborated by Wikipedia and Virgilio Sapere, note rewritten. (2) SOURCE URL WAS WRONG — Claude had cited Treccani's 'occhio' entry; the locuzione is listed under CROCE. Now cites croce, plus De Mauro's dedicated headword. Both still unopened by a human: verifiedOn stays null. IT reviewer: confirm the kitchen/estimate split, the only claim still unsupported.
